nativeRange.habitat: Coastal streams, rivers, and estuaries; tolerates soft acidic blackwater inland as well as slightly brackish coastal waters near river mouths

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, Amazon sword, hornwort, floating plants for diffused lighting)
dark substrate to enhance translucent body coloration
driftwood and leaf litter for tannin release (optional)
open midwater swimming areas for schooling movement

diet.notes: Readily accepts high-quality micro-pellets, small flakes, and a variety of frozen or freeze-dried foods including brine shrimp, daphnia, and bloodworms. Will also take micro-worms and other small live foods. Varied diet maintains bright coloration. Small mouth size requires appropriately sized food particles.

goodWith: other small peaceful tetras (neon tetra, ember tetra, rummy-nose tetra)
avoidWith: large cichlids and other aggressive fish
large carnivorous fish that can swallow them whole

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo of Pristella maxillaris showing translucent body with characteristic yellow-tipped fins and black-and-white banding.

summary: The X-Ray Tetra is a small, peaceful schooling fish named for its translucent body through which internal organs and the spine are visible. Hardy and adaptable, it tolerates a wide pH range and even mildly brackish water, making it an ideal beginner tetra. Distinctive yellow-tipped dorsal and anal fins with bold black-and-white bands.
careNotes:
The X-Ray Tetra (Pristella maxillaris) is one of the hardiest characids available in the hobby and is recommended for beginners. Its common name derives from the translucent body wall through which the spine, swim bladder, and internal organs are clearly visible — a feature shared by few other aquarium tetras. In the wild it inhabits coastal river systems and estuaries of Venezuela, Guyana, and northern Brazil, including the Orinoco and Amazon drainages, and is notably euryhaline — tolerating slightly brackish conditions near river mouths. This brackish tolerance is unusual among tetras and means it can be kept successfully across a wide salinity range, though pure freshwater is ideal for community tanks.

A group of at least six individuals is strongly recommended; smaller groups result in stress and faded coloration. In a well-planted tank with a dark substrate, the yellow-tipped fins with their contrasting black-and-white banding become particularly striking. The species accepts a wide pH range (6.0–8.0), making it compatible with both soft acidic setups and harder alkaline community tanks — a flexibility rarely found among Characidae.

The X-Ray Tetra should not be confused with the Glass Tetra (Moenkhausia oligolepis) or the Glass Bloodfin (Prionobrama filigera), both of which also display translucent bodies but belong to different genera and have distinct care requirements. The albino form of P. maxillaris is occasionally available in the trade and shows the same fin patterning on a white body.
breedingNotes: Egg scatterers that will breed readily in a dedicated spawning tank. Use a bare-bottom tank or fine-leaved plants (Java moss, spawning mops) to collect eggs, as adults will consume eggs and fry. Condition breeders with frequent feedings of live or frozen foods. Water temperature 26–28°C, pH 6.5–7.0, and soft water (gH 2–8) trigger spawning. Females scatter 300–400 adhesive eggs among plants; remove adults immediately after spawning. Eggs hatch in 24–36 hours; fry become free-swimming after another 3–4 days. Feed with infusoria or commercial fry foods initially, then transition to baby brine shrimp and micro-worms as fry grow. Breeding difficulty is moderate as the setup requires a separate tank and careful egg protection.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Pristella maxillaris
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (3.5–4.5 cm), temperature range (24–28°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness, native range (Orinoco, Amazon coastal drainages, Venezuela, Guyana, Brazil), euryhaline habitat notes, diet, compatibility, and breeding information.

additional.name: FishBase — Pristella maxillaris
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Characidae, Characiformes), adult size (to 4.5 cm TL), native distribution (Venezuela, Guyana, Brazil), temperature (24–28°C), and euryhaline/brackish habitat data. Confirmed the species is found in coastal drainages including the Amazon and Orinoco systems.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — X-Ray Tetra Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade care guide confirming beginner suitability, minimum group size of 6, tank size (15–20 gal / ~57–76 L), omnivorous diet, peaceful temperament, and compatibility with other small community fish. Noted wide pH and temperature tolerance.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Pristella maxillaris profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source confirming brackish tolerance, lifespan (3–5 years), fin coloration description (yellow tips, black-and-white banding on dorsal and anal fins), and note that the species is not to be confused with other transparent-body characids such as the Glass Bloodfin (Prionobrama filigera).

additional.name: Seriously Fish — Pristella maxillaris (breeding section)
additional.notes: Additional breeding detail: egg-scatterer spawning behaviour, egg count (~300–400), incubation period (24–36 h), fry development timeline, and water conditioning triggers for spawning.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterer; requires separate spawning tank with fine-leaved plants or spawning mops. Adults must be removed immediately after spawning. Fry initially fed infusoria, transitioning to baby brine shrimp. Soft, slightly acidic water at 26–28°C triggers spawning.
